attributes.stem:
A 70-year-old man presents with a painless swelling in the right side of his neck. He first noticed the swelling about two weeks ago and mentions that it has gradually increased in size since then. There is no associated pain, difficulty swallowing, or changes in his voice. He has not experienced any recent infections or trauma to the area. Over the last few months he has noticed fatigue, some night sweats and about 5 kg of unexplained weight loss . Additionally, the patient mentions feeling unwell with a non-specific viral illness in the recent past.

The patient has a history of well-managed hypertension, treated with amlodipine. He has a 20 pack-year smoking history and has a glass of wine most evenings.

On physical examination, a well defined lump firm is clearly visible (Image). The mass measures approximately 3 cm in diameter, is mobile and the skin moves freely over it. No other masses are evident and the rest of the physical examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Based on the patient's symptoms and the presence of an enlarged lymph node in the right posterior triangle of the neck, the most likely diagnosis for this 70-year-old man is non-Hodgkin lymphoma. This cancer often presents with painless lymph node enlargement. In this case, the patient's firm, mobile mass measuring approximately 3 cm in diameter in the right cervical lymph node region and no other lymph nodes are palpable in the neck suggests a localised enlargement rather than a systemic infection. Other clinical features such as the patient's age and the presence of associated symptoms of fatigue, night sweats and unexplained weight loss are also suggestive of a possible lymphoproliferative disorder like non-Hodgkin lymphoma.

Infectious mononucleosis is a viral illness primarily caused by the Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), a member of the herpesvirus family. This condition typically presents with a triad of symptoms: fever, sore throat, and lymphadenopathy. The onset is often acute, with symptoms progressively worsening over several days. Common clinical manifestations include fatigue, malaise, and generalised discomfort. Patients may complain of significant pharyngitis with exudative tonsillitis, and the lymph nodes, particularly those in the cervical region, are frequently enlarged and tender. In this case the chronic nature of the symptoms, the absence of hepatosplenomegaly, and the rapid progression seen in the presented case make infectious mononucleosis a less likely diagnosis. This condition typically follows a more acute and self-limiting course, in contrast to the persistent and progressive nature of lymphoproliferative disorders like lymphomas.

Whilst a diagnosis of carcinoma of the lung should always be considered in a smoker who presents with systemic symptoms and weight loss, when these tumours do metastasise to the neck, it is usually the left supraclavicular nodes (Virchow's node) that are involved first. Lung carcinoma would typically present with respiratory symptoms, which are notably absent in this scenario.

Lipomas are benign, slow-growing fatty tumours and are not associated with systemic symptoms. Most lipomas are found on the trunk and the neck would be rather an unusual site. They are not usually as well defined as this particular lesion.

Epidermoid cysts are common findings in the neck. They are attached to the skin and the physical description for this lump would not fit for such a diagnosis.
learningPoints: Consider non-Hodgkin lymphoma in a patient with painless cervical lymph node enlargement and constitutional symptoms.
references: Sapkota S, Shaikh H. Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma. [Updated 2023 Feb 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-.
Balm AJ, van Velthuysen ML, Hoebers FJ, Vogel WV, van den Brekel MW. Diagnosis and treatment of a neck node swelling suspicious for a malignancy: an algorithmic approach. Int J Surg Oncol. 2010.
